keyConcepts.label: Dimensions and entries
keyConcepts.microLesson: A matrix is just a rectangular table of numbers. Its **dimensions** are always stated rows-by-columns: a matrix with 2 rows and 3 columns is a $2 \times 3$ matrix. The **entry** $a_{ij}$ sits in row $i$, column $j$ — row first, column second. In $\begin{bmatrix} 5 & 8 \\ 1 & 9 \end{bmatrix}$, the entry $a_{21} = 1$ (second row, first column). Getting the order right matters: $a_{12}$ and $a_{21}$ are usually different numbers.

keyConcepts.label: Adding and subtracting matrices
keyConcepts.microLesson: To add or subtract two matrices, just add or subtract the numbers in matching positions — entry by entry. This only works when both matrices have the **same dimensions**; otherwise the operation is undefined. $\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 0 \\ 1 & 6 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 6 & 2 \\ 4 & 10 \end{bmatrix}$. It's exactly how you'd total two spreadsheets cell by cell.

keyConcepts.label: Scalar multiplication
keyConcepts.microLesson: Multiplying a matrix by a single number (a **scalar**) multiplies *every* entry by that number. $3 \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 0 & 4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 6 & 3 \\ 0 & 12 \end{bmatrix}$. This lets you build **linear combinations** like $2A - 3B$: scale each matrix first, then add or subtract entry by entry. Scaling a whole price table by $1.1$ to apply a 10% markup is exactly this operation.

keyConcepts.label: Operations on real data
keyConcepts.microLesson: Real data often lives in matrices: rows for products, columns for stores; rows for players, columns for games. **Adding** two such tables totals them (this month + last month). **Scaling** applies a uniform rate (double every order, add tax). **Combinations** like $A - B$ find a change or difference. The arithmetic is identical to abstract matrices — the entries just carry meaning like 'units of cereal at Store 2'.

lecture.body:
## Tables of numbers you can do arithmetic on

A spreadsheet of sales — products down the side, stores across the top — is already a **matrix**: a rectangular grid of numbers. Matrix operations are the rules for combining whole tables at once, so instead of adding 200 cells by hand you write $A + B$ and mean it precisely. That is why matrices run everything from inventory systems to image filters to the math behind 3D graphics.

## Dimensions and entries: addresses in the grid

Every matrix has **dimensions** written rows $\times$ columns. This table

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 8 & 2 \\ 1 & 9 & 4 \end{bmatrix}$$

is a $2 \times 3$ matrix: 2 rows, 3 columns. A single number inside is an **entry**, addressed as $a_{ij}$ — **row $i$ first, column $j$ second**. So $a_{13} = 2$ (row 1, column 3) and $a_{21} = 1$ (row 2, column 1). Order is not optional: $a_{12}$ and $a_{21}$ generally name different cells.

A matrix with the same number of rows and columns (like $3 \times 3$) is called **square**.

## Addition and subtraction: cell by cell

To add two matrices, add the entries in **matching positions**. To subtract, subtract them.

$$\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 0 \\ 1 & 6 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 6 & 2 \\ 4 & 10 \end{bmatrix}$$

The one rule: the matrices must have the **same dimensions**. You can't add a $2 \times 2$ to a $2 \times 3$ — there's no partner for the extra column, so the sum is **undefined**.

## Scalar multiplication: scale everything at once

A **scalar** is a plain number. Multiplying a matrix by a scalar multiplies **every entry**:

$$3 \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 0 & 4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 6 & 3 \\ 0 & 12 \end{bmatrix}$$

This is how you apply a uniform rate to a whole table — a 10% markup is multiplication by $1.1$; doubling an order is multiplication by $2$.

## Linear combinations: the two tools together

Most real formulas mix scaling and adding. To compute $2A - 3B$, **scale each matrix first, then combine entry by entry**:

$$2\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 0 & 5 \end{bmatrix} - 3\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 2 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 4 \\ 0 & 10 \end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 0 \\ 6 & 3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 4 \\ -6 & 7 \end{bmatrix}$$

That single expression might mean *"two weeks of the first store's sales minus three times a baseline"* — the whole comparison done in one line.

## Why the same-dimension rule matters

Addition and subtraction only make sense when the tables line up. If two inventory reports don't have the same products in the same rows, adding them is meaningless — and the math agrees by leaving the operation undefined. Checking dimensions first is the matrix version of *checking units* before you combine quantities.

application.summary: Matrix addition totals two data tables (this month + last month, Store A + Store B); scalar multiplication applies a uniform rate (markup, tax, doubling an order); and combinations like A - B reveal change or difference. The same-dimension rule is just the requirement that the tables line up.
application.workedExample:
**Combining inventory across two stores**

A chain tracks units of three products (rows: cereal, milk, eggs) at each store (this is one column per store). Store A's stock is $\begin{bmatrix} 40 \\ 25 \\ 60 \end{bmatrix}$ and Store B's is $\begin{bmatrix} 30 \\ 50 \\ 20 \end{bmatrix}$.

**Total stock** across both stores is the sum, added row by row:

$$\begin{bmatrix} 40 \\ 25 \\ 60 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 30 \\ 50 \\ 20 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 70 \\ 75 \\ 80 \end{bmatrix}$$

Suppose headquarters wants every store to **double** its cereal-milk-eggs holdings for a promotion. That's scalar multiplication by 2: Store A would need $2\begin{bmatrix} 40 \\ 25 \\ 60 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 80 \\ 50 \\ 120 \end{bmatrix}$. The same two moves — add to total, scale to adjust — drive almost every inventory, payroll, and scorekeeping system.
domainNotes: **Retail/inventory:** add stock matrices across stores or weeks; scale by a reorder multiplier or markup.
**Business:** combine cost and revenue tables; apply a uniform tax or discount rate to a price matrix.
**Sports analytics:** add per-game stat tables to get season totals; subtract to compare two players.
**Data analysis:** subtract a baseline matrix from current data to get the change in every cell at once.

problems.prompt: Consider the matrix $A = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 8 & 2 \\ 1 & 9 & 4 \end{bmatrix}$. State its dimensions.

steps.instruction: How many rows does $A$ have?
steps.hint: Count the horizontal lines of numbers.

steps.instruction: How many columns does $A$ have?
steps.hint: Count the vertical lines of numbers.

problems.solutionNarrative: There are 2 horizontal rows and 3 vertical columns, so $A$ is a $2 \times 3$ matrix.

problems.prompt: Let $A = \begin{bmatrix} 7 & 3 & 0 \\ 2 & 6 & 9 \end{bmatrix}$. Read off the requested entries (row first, column second).

steps.instruction: What is the entry $a_{13}$ (row 1, column 3)?
steps.hint: Go to row 1, then count over to the third column.

steps.instruction: What is the entry $a_{21}$ (row 2, column 1)?
What is the entry $a_{22}$ (row 2, column 2)?

problems.prompt: A matrix $B$ has dimensions $4 \times 5$. Answer the following about it.

steps.instruction: How many entries does $B$ have in total?
steps.hint: A grid with 4 rows and 5 columns has rows × columns cells.

steps.instruction: Is $B$ a square matrix? Answer 'yes' or 'no'.
steps.hint: A matrix is square only when its number of rows equals its number of columns.

steps.instruction: In the address $b_{ij}$, which index gives the row — the first or the second?
steps.hint: The convention is always row first, column second.

finalAnswer.value: 20 entries; not square; row is first

problems.solutionNarrative: A $4 \times 5$ matrix has $4 \times 5 = 20$ entries. Since $4 \ne 5$ it is not square, and in $b_{ij}$ the first index $i$ is always the row.

steps.instruction: Add the matrices entry by entry. Give the result as [[a, b], [c, d]].
Subtract entry by entry. Give the result as [[a, b], [c, d]].
What are the dimensions of the first matrix? Answer as 'rows x columns', e.g. 2 x 2.
What are the dimensions of the second matrix?
Is this sum defined? Answer 'yes' or 'no'.

problems.solutionNarrative: The matrices are $2 \times 2$ and $2 \times 3$. Because the dimensions differ, there is no entry-by-entry partner for the extra column, so the sum is undefined.

steps.instruction: Multiply every entry by 3. Give the result as [[a, b], [c, d]].
Multiply every entry by $-2$, watching the signs. Give the result as [[a, b], [c, d]].
First compute $2A$. Give it as [[a, b], [c, d]].
Now compute $3B$. Give it as [[a, b], [c, d]].
Subtract: $2A - 3B$. Give the result as [[a, b], [c, d]].

problems.prompt: A bakery tracks units sold of (cupcakes, cookies) on two days as column matrices. Monday sold $\begin{bmatrix} 12 \\ 30 \end{bmatrix}$ and Tuesday sold $\begin{bmatrix} 18 \\ 25 \end{bmatrix}$. Find the two-day total.

steps.instruction: Add the two columns entry by entry. Give the result as [[a], [b]].

problems.solutionNarrative: Adding the columns: cupcakes $12 + 18 = 30$, cookies $30 + 25 = 55$, giving $\begin{bmatrix} 30 \\ 55 \end{bmatrix}$.

problems.prompt: A store's inventory of (shirts, pants) by size sits in $\begin{bmatrix} 10 & 8 \\ 6 & 4 \end{bmatrix}$ (rows = product, columns = size). The manager doubles every quantity for a sale. Find the new inventory.

steps.instruction: Multiply the whole matrix by 2. Give the result as [[a, b], [c, d]].

problems.solutionNarrative: Scalar multiplication by 2 doubles each entry: $\begin{bmatrix} 20 & 16 \\ 12 & 8 \end{bmatrix}$.

problems.prompt: Two stores' stock of three products are columns. Store A: $\begin{bmatrix} 40 \\ 25 \\ 60 \end{bmatrix}$, Store B: $\begin{bmatrix} 30 \\ 50 \\ 20 \end{bmatrix}$. Headquarters wants the combined chain-wide total.

steps.instruction: Add the two store columns entry by entry. Give the result as [[a], [b], [c]].

problems.solutionNarrative: Chain-wide total is the sum: $40+30=70$, $25+50=75$, $60+20=80$, giving $\begin{bmatrix} 70 \\ 75 \\ 80 \end{bmatrix}$.

problems.prompt: A cafe's wholesale price matrix (dollars) for two items at two suppliers is $\begin{bmatrix} 4 & 5 \\ 2 & 3 \end{bmatrix}$. The cafe marks everything up to triple the wholesale price for the menu. Find the menu-price matrix.

steps.instruction: Multiply the price matrix by 3. Give the result as [[a, b], [c, d]].

problems.solutionNarrative: Tripling each price by scalar multiplication: $\begin{bmatrix} 12 & 15 \\ 6 & 9 \end{bmatrix}$.

problems.prompt: A player's stats (rows: points, rebounds) over two games are $\begin{bmatrix} 20 \\ 8 \end{bmatrix}$ and $\begin{bmatrix} 16 \\ 12 \end{bmatrix}$. A teammate's totals over the same two games are $\begin{bmatrix} 30 \\ 14 \end{bmatrix}$. By how much do the first player's two-game totals trail the teammate's, stat by stat?

steps.instruction: First find the first player's two-game total by adding their two game columns. Give it as [[a], [b]].
Now subtract the first player's total from the teammate's total to get the gap. Give it as [[a], [b]].

problems.solutionNarrative: The first player's totals are $\begin{bmatrix} 36 \\ 20 \end{bmatrix}$. The teammate minus the player gives $\begin{bmatrix} 30-36 \\ 14-20 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -6 \\ 6 \end{bmatrix}$: the player actually scored 6 more points but grabbed 6 fewer rebounds.

problems.prompt: Last month's sales matrix (rows: products, columns: two stores) was $A = \begin{bmatrix} 10 & 6 \\ 4 & 8 \end{bmatrix}$. This month's was $B = \begin{bmatrix} 14 & 6 \\ 9 & 5 \end{bmatrix}$. Management projects next month at twice this month's sales plus last month's, i.e. $2B + A$. Find the projection.

steps.instruction: First compute $2B$. Give it as [[a, b], [c, d]].
Now add $A$ to get $2B + A$. Give the result as [[a, b], [c, d]].
First compute $2B$. Give it as [[a, b, c], [d, e, f]].
Now add $A$ to get $A + 2B$. Give the result as [[a, b, c], [d, e, f]].
Is $A$ a square matrix? Answer 'yes' or 'no'.
steps.hint: It is square when rows equal columns.

steps.instruction: Compute $A + A$. Give the result as [[a, b], [c, d]].
steps.hint: Adding $A$ to itself doubles every entry.

problems.solutionNarrative: $A$ is $2 \times 2$, so it is square. Adding $A$ to itself doubles each entry: $\begin{bmatrix} 18 & 2 \\ 8 & 14 \end{bmatrix}$.
